And ? What if that's not enough ?
So that group votes and hopes that the result goes their way ? Anyone can look at any problem and say "then you just vote". Heck you could say that right now to everyone about literally any issue out there, but that's not a good argument, is it ?

message:
>Communism was ineffective in the past, but would thrive in contemporary society due to increased automation

Why do you believe that the fundamental problems that existed in all the past iterations of communism that aren't to do with automation will be rectified this time around?

Even if a vast majority of jobs were automated, the ownership structure of all of the production from that automation would need to be managed efficiently and effectively. Why would the community to a better, more efficient job of doing that than the free market would? 

It seems to be that the reason that we're arriving at the level of automation we currently have is due to free market innovation. Automation seems to be a direct consequence of that, which is directly attributable to capitalism. 

Why not solve the problems associated with automation within the capitalist system that created it than traverse to an entirely different, unproven system that has the single worst track record of pretty much any economic system every conceived? 

And further to that,

>Any unspecialized labor that can’t be automated would be distributed evenly among the community

What if I refuse to do this labor?

"@deafening-sweep, @thick-sync, you guys are just drinking the capitalist Kool-Aid. Automation is gonna free us from the shackles of boring jobs and let us pursue our passions. And if you don't like it, well, you can just vote against it, right? I mean, what's the worst that could happen? You'll have to actually contribute to society or something?"

"@primary-click, oh man, you're so extra. 'Drinking the capitalist Kool-Aid'? Really? At least @deafening-sweep and @thick-sync are bringing up actual points about the potential downsides of communism. You're just regurgitating some utopian fantasy. What about the people who don't want to 'contribute to society' in the way that you deem fit? What about individual freedom?"

> Why is paying a tax immoral?

Because IMHO it's not a tax, It's a penalty. The first of it's kind ever in the sense that it is the first time in history that a citizen has had to pay for not purchasing a product. Again, I fully understand that the SCOTUS is not on my side here, It's just how I feel.

I'm self-employed. I can't afford 1/3rd of my income going to insurance. I just can't...

>I did not imply that jokes against men are offensive to me, I am rather egalitarian in that regard and think nobody should get all worked up over jokes.

Then why bring up the jokes against men as a reason for you being egalitarian if it didn't bother you?

> You are being triggered and having psychosomatic responses to jokes and my opinion?

No, I find this whole exchange really amusing, especially with you condescending to me while _completely failing to understand my point_. Stress and anxiety can trigger physical sensations, that's basic biology. To skip ahead for a moment to illustrate my point:

>What is it that is causing you stress and anxiety to the point of illness that Republicans in Congress are doing? I am not a big fan of the government, but I have been trying to figure this out for a while. What exactly is it that is making you sick with worry?

I'm a diabetic. If the Healthcare bill that passed the House passes the Senate, the analysis of the bill says that I could expect a $5000 premium hike if community rating is removed. I'm trying to move in with my significant other, who is still in school and can't work full time, so I'll be expected to take on more than his share of the bills. With my savings, we can just barely make it until he graduates and get a job, but with a $5000 premium hike, we'd be underwater. That _stresses me out_, I get headaches and sick feelings worrying about whether we'll be able to make ends meet or whether I'd have to skimp on healthcare _that I need_.

So, going back to the above point, I know how a _difference of opinion_ can be threatening to _my_ health, so I'm more empathetic to the claim that a difference of opinion can affect other people's health.

>In your opinion, are you sensitive because you are female?

First of all, I'm a guy. A white guy. A white guy who can make jokes about how the world would be better if we killed off all white men because we're so terrible without getting triggered by it. Second of all, I'm "so sensitive" because I have _empathy_ for *the struggles of other people*. Because rather than tell people why they feel the way they do and why they're wrong, I listen to them and try to understand where they're coming from.

Why I am egalitarian:
I was raised by a single mother. 

I have had several bosses who were female. 

I interact with female decision makers and executives every day.

My best friends have been female. 

My divorce attorney was female.

My doctor is female.

My dentist is female.

My professional wife, and female cousins are all equal.

Several of my girlfriends were 2nd wave feminist and/or bisexual.

I do not think women should be torn down.

I strongly support 2nd wave feminism.

I believe that an intelligent and hard working women is equal to an intelligent hard working man.

I earned where I am and know women are just as capable and empowered to do the same.


Why I am not a modern feminist:

It has been co-opted as a political movement. I belong to no political party.

It is anti-abortion. I am also anti-abortion, but I understand that some people find it murder and think it is no reason to exclude from feminism.

Stupid pink hats do not say, "Take me seriously"

2nd wave feminism was about equality, 3rd wave seems to be about revenge.

Man hating jokes are in style, I do not want anyone to be hated.

Too easy to be "triggered". Women should be stronger and better than constantly being "triggered".

I worked shit jobs to support my family, right along women and think they could all do it.

NOW refused to call out people who used rape threats and vulgar slang against Sarah Palin. (I am not a fan of hers, but that whole rights have no party thing..)

I know some great and strong Republican women who are not allowed in the womens rights movement.

I do not think men should be torn down to make women stronger.

Do the motives really matter? If you do a bunch of racist shit, you're a racist. I'm not sure what difference it makes that Trump's motives are unknown, and you're asking for a pretty unreasonable standard of proof. NONE of us knows what Trump actually thinks about [insert race here] in his conscious or subconscious mind. We can only judge him by his actions. And some of his actions, like those just listed by /u/MikeyPWhatAG , were pretty racist. 

>No white supremacist would date a black girl, certainly not proudly and in public. Trump did. 

Why not? I think you're confusing "white supremacist" with "KKK member" here. It's absolutely possible for a person who harbors some white supremacist beliefs to publicly date a black person, though.

>No white supremacist would literally condemn racism and white supremacy. Trump does, consistently.

Again, plenty of white supremacists would do this. Also, I don't think "consistently" is a fair characterization of Trump's actions here. He consistently SAYS racism is bad, yes. But if you look at (for example) his responses to Charlottesville, I think you'll have a tough time arguing that he was sending a consistent "white supremacy is bad" message there. I mean, he said there were nice people in a crowd of white supremacists that was waving Nazi flags and chanting "blood and soil." There are many nice Trump supporters, I'm sure, but I don't see how there could be any nice people in that crowd. 

(I mean, say you attended the rally based on a misunderstanding of what it was about...would YOU have stuck around when you started seeing the Nazi flags? Would any reasonable person?)

>No white supremacist would be seen with a daughter who married a jew. Kushner is one of Trump's close advisors. 

See above bit about dating black people. 

>No white supremacist would guest star on The Fresh Prince of Bel Air. Trump did.

....what? Why not? That's just a way to make some cash and promote your brand to a different market. You can definitely be a white supremacist and still be interested in making money off of black people. But anyway, whatever white supremacist beliefs Trump holds, I think it's clear that his ego and drive for self-promotion overrides pretty much everything else, so it's not surprising at all that he'd do something like this even if he *consciously* hated black people (which I don't think he does). 

>No white supremacist would say things like, "a heart full of patriotism leaves no room for hatred". Trump does.

message:
>You seriously think that white supremacists date outside their race? 

Absolutely. To be clear though, I don't mean "white supremacists" in the sense of somebody who has a collection of Nazi memorabilia at home. I'm talking about people who hold, *consciously or even subconsciously*, some white supremacist beliefs. 

>When your whole organizing emotion is "save the white race, miscegenation is a plot by <evil minority> to destroy us"... you don't do that.

I agree, but there are many people who hold white supremacist beliefs or do things to support white supremacy but don't have that as their organizing emotion. Sometimes they may not even be conscious of it (for example, the employer who calls back fewer "black" names for job interviews), and in many cases it can be a reflection more of ignorance than of malice or evil. 

>Because it's a non sequitur. There are tons of reasons one could have for believing or touting the birther conspiracy that aren't racist.

You could say this about literally any racist action. Hypothetically, there are tons of reasons you could have for burning a cross on the lawn of a black family, too. Maybe you just hate crosses. Maybe it's chilly and you're trying to keep them warm. Maybe crosses are banned under the HOA agreement and you're trying to do them a favor before somebody from the board sees it. Maybe you think the ash is going to help fertilize their lawn.

If your standard for racism is that the person has to come out and say "I am doing this purely for racial reasons" then you're right, Donald Trump is not a racist. Nor are the cops who shoot unarmed, innocent black people. Nor is pretty much anyone aside from a very small group of incredibly hardcore people who see racism as a core part of their identity. By that definition, racism exists pretty much only on Stormfront and in a tiny minority of crimes each year. 

I don't think that's how most people - and certainly not most minorities - would define racism, though.

This comes up occasionally on here. The difference between the two is that FGM serves no legitimate medical purpose. There is data that shows circumcision does have medical benefit, with the strongest data being that it reduces UTI’s.
personality_characteristics: ['Has a knack for creating unique art installations', 'Active', 'Democrat', 'Supports policies on environmental protection and arts funding']

message:
[There have been similar studies which show medical benefits for female circumcision](https://anthrosource.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1525/maq.2007.21.3.301) (including a lower prevalence in HIV infection, among others).

The reality is that just like male circumcision, the reason it was started and continued has nothing to do with any medical benefits. The people who wanted to continue it desperately searched for any and all possible benefit, no matter how slight, in order to push the continuation of circumcision.

>the strongest data being that it reduces UTI’s.

For the record, the studies which purport to show this have quite a few methodological issues. But even if we ignored those issues and accepted their conclusions, [**you would need to mutilate the genitals of 111 to 195 babies in order to prevent a single UTI.**](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15890696)

That seems like a bit much in order to prevent a condition which is easily and simply treated with oral antibiotics. Especially given that other, far better studies which controlled for more confounding factors did not see any connection between circumcision and UTI rates. 

Circumcision is not a risk-free medical procedure. Boys do indeed die every year in the USA due to botched circumcisions and the risks associated with any surgical procedure. They lose their genitalia totally, have to have reconstructive surgeries, suffer from erectile dysfunction, and more.

@champagne-transmission, I see your point about the methodological issues in those studies, but don't you think it's unfair to dismiss the potential benefits of circumcision entirely? I'm not saying I'm for or against it, but it seems like there's still a lot we don't know. What are your thoughts on the long-term effects, @wan-korat?

@inverse-support, I think @champagne-transmission brought up some solid points about the methodological issues, but I also see what you're saying. It's a complex issue and we shouldn't dismiss potential benefits outright. @wan-korat, what's your take on the long-term effects of circumcision? Do you think the benefits outweigh the risks?

@inverse-support, @olive-base, I think you're both missing the point. We're not just talking about potential benefits, we're talking about the fact that the benefits are extremely minimal and the risks are very real. And let's not forget, we're talking about performing a surgical procedure on a newborn baby without their consent. I'm not convinced that the potential benefits, which are still debatable, outweigh the very real risks of complications and long-term effects. Eliminating the deduction for taxes paid only hurts people who make enough money and have enough deductions to itemize their deductions (25% of Americans). That means, if you pay $5,000 in property taxes and $2,000 in state income taxes, you get to deduct $7,000 from your federal taxable income. States that have the highest state income and property taxes; CA, NY, NJ, MA, CT, IL, would see the tax burden shifted to them the most. Notice those are all blue states. This isn't about wealthy vs. non-wealthy, this about Democratic representatives protecting their constituents, who are mostly upper and upper-middle class homeowners in the top 20%, from tax increases.
